summary: I decided to join the United States Marine Corps in 1944 because I didn't want to be in the Army or Navy. I went through boot camp at Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego and experienced the tough training there. During my time, I was part of the 27th, 31st, and 34th training battalions on the West Coast. The Marine Corps shaped my early adult life and gave me a sense of purpose.
cues: Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego

summary: My brother Marv was drafted into the United States Army right after high school graduation. He initially joined the Signal Corps, then was moved to the Tank unit, and later to the Air Corps before washing out of pilot training. Eventually, he ended up serving in Patton's Army during the North Africa Campaign. The Army was a big part of my family's military experience during World War II.

I didn't want to join the United States Navy, so I chose the Marine Corps instead. However, I did encounter a shore patrol sailor while returning from furlough, who helped me with traveler's orders when I was three days late getting back. The Navy's shore patrol played a role in maintaining order among servicemen during that time.

Arnie Martin was a kid I used to play ball with, and we joined the Marine Corps together in 1944. While I was sent to San Diego for boot camp, Arnie was sent to Camp Lejeune and then to Parris Island. We started this journey together, but our paths diverged based on the Marine Corps' assignments.

Camp Lejeune was where Arnie Martin was sent after we joined the Marine Corps, while I was sent to San Diego. It was one of the main Marine Corps training locations during World War II. I didn't train there myself, but it was part of the Marine Corps infrastructure that shaped many recruits.

entity: Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego
summary: I went through boot camp at the Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego starting in August 1944. The training was rough but expected, and it prepared me for my service. After boot camp, I had a ten-day furlough to return home to Fond du Lac before returning to duty.

Parris Island was where Arnie Martin was sent for his Marine Corps training instead of San Diego. It was one of the main recruit depots on the East Coast. Although I didn't train there, Parris Island was a key part of the Marine Corps' training system during World War II.

Fond du Lac, Wisconsin, was my hometown where I spent time before joining the Marine Corps. I went to vocational school there for a year before shipping out for boot camp. After boot camp, I came back on a ten-day furlough to Fond du Lac, where my family lived and where I attended my uncle's funeral.

Milwaukee was where I went to take my physical exam before joining the Marine Corps. I also boarded a troop train there that took me to boot camp. It was a key transit point for me in August 1944 as I began my military service.

After leaving Milwaukee, the troop train I was on stopped in Chicago before continuing south to San Diego. The train cars were old and rough, but it was part of the journey to boot camp. Chicago was one of the major cities we passed through on the way to training.

My brother Marv initially joined the Signal Corps in the United States Army because of his good IQ. He later transferred to the Tank unit and then to the Air Corps. The Signal Corps was his first assignment after being drafted right out of high school.

My brother Marv was transferred into the United States Army Air Forces after starting in the Signal Corps and Tank units. He went to Rock Island, Illinois, and then to Texas for pilot training but washed out. This was during the time of the North Africa Campaign when the Army was heavily engaged overseas.

Rock Island, Illinois, was where my brother Marv spent about a year or two of college while in the Army Air Forces. It was part of his training before going to Texas for pilot training. Rock Island was a key location in his military education during World War II.

The North Africa Campaign was a major event during World War II when my brother Marv was in the Army. He ended up serving with Patton's Army after being in the Tank unit. The campaign was brutal, and the Sherman tanks they used were like tin cans, very vulnerable in battle.

My brother Marv served in tanks during World War II, specifically Sherman tanks. He said they were like tin cans, very vulnerable in combat, especially during the North Africa Campaign. The tanks were not the safest place to be, which is why he was encouraged to transfer to the Air Corps.

While returning from my furlough, I encountered a shore patrol sailor on the train. He helped me by writing traveler's orders when I was three days late getting back to duty. The shore patrol maintained order and enforced regulations among servicemen during that time.

During my time in the Marine Corps, the 27th Marine Regiment was one of the training battalions on the West Coast. Many of us were out on ten-day furlough transfers while the officers were away. The 27th was part of the structure that trained Marines like me in 1944.

The 31st Marine Regiment was another training battalion on the West Coast where I was stationed during my Marine Corps service. Like the 27th and 34th, they handled many recruits and were part of the Marine Corps' training system in 1944. We often had furloughs while the officers were away.

The 34th Marine Regiment was one of the three training battalions on the West Coast during my time in the Marine Corps. Along with the 27th and 31st, they were responsible for training new Marines in 1944. Many of us were out on furloughs during that period while the officers were off duty.
